exemplar.title: Investigating the Effects of Different Amounts of Potassium Chloride (KCl) on the Growth of Basil (Ocimum bascilicum) Seedlings

detailedExemplarData.title: Investigating the Effects of Different Amounts of Potassium Chloride (KCl) on the Growth of Basil (Ocimum bascilicum) Seedlings

subject.title: Biology
subject.description:
**IB Biology** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **four interconnected themes: Unity and Diversity, Form and Function, Interaction and Interdependence, and Continuity and Change**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, scientific inquiry, data analysis, and practical investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice and data-based questions | 1 hour 20 min | 2 hours | 36% |
| **Paper 2** | Short and extended response questions | 1 hour 30 min | 2 hours 30 min | 44% |
| **Scientific Investigation** | Individual research project | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

files.fileName: (Final) Biology Internal Assessment - Zoie Lu copy.pdf

strengths: Research question clearly identifies independent and dependent variables within a specific agronomic context.
Background effectively links basil physiology and KCl fertiliser practice, justifying experimental concentrations and duration.
Methodological considerations are well explained, with discussion of key variables and rationale for replicates and timeframe.
weaknesses: Procedural ambiguities remain (inconsistent cup numbers, omitted time-zero measurements).
No seed viability test described to ensure uniform germination.
Volume-based KCl percentages lack conversion to mass, reducing reproducibility clarity.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work demonstrates a specific and appropriate context and explains how to collect relevant and sufficient data, meeting top-band descriptors. Minor omissions in procedural detail and reproducibility keep the description from a flawless 6, warranting a score of 5.

strengths: Raw data are comprehensively tabulated with means, standard deviations, and a scatter plot with error bars.
Appropriate statistical processing (one-way ANOVA with Tukey HSD) demonstrates sound analysis.
Clarity in descriptive statistics is shown via inclusion of skewness formula and consolidated summary tables.
weaknesses: Table headings and unit placement are misaligned; integer data obscure the stated Â±0.05 cm uncertainty.
Uncertainties are listed but not propagated into error bars or reflected in processed results.
Minor inaccuracies in SD/skewness values and missing F-statistic/df in ANOVA reduce precision.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data recording and processing are communicated clearly, and relevant analyses are performed accurately for the most part. Consideration of uncertainties shows significant omissions. Overall performance aligns with the 3â4 band, justifying a score of 4.

strengths: Conclusion directly answers the research question and is quantitatively supported by the statistical results (e.g., p < 0.00001).
Findings are explained using accepted physiological concepts and literature on potassium and salinity stress.
No contradictory claims are made; conclusion is consistent with all presented analysis.
weaknesses: Variation in SD and skewness is not addressed in the discussion of results.
Comparison to scientific context is relevant but lacks deeper critical integration with multiple sources.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is justified, relevant, and fully consistent with the analysis, meeting top-band criteria. The scientific context comparison is solid but not exhaustively critical, placing the performance in the upper middle band and warranting a score of 5.

strengths: Identifies specific methodological weaknesses (measurement error, environmental fluctuations, incomplete dissolution).
Proposes realistic improvements directly related to issues (narrower concentration intervals, longer duration).
weaknesses: Does not explain the relative impact or magnitude of each identified weakness.
Improvements are described but not linked in detail to how they mitigate specific limitations.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The report describes specific methodological weaknesses and gives realistic improvements, aligning with the middle-band descriptors. However, lacking depth in explaining the relative impact of errors and the mitigating role of each improvement confines the score to 3.

strandSelections.reasoning: The conclusion directly answers the research question, quantitatively summarises trends, and explicitly links these statements to statistical results (ANOVA p < 0.00001).  Assertions are fully consistent with the processed data and no contradictory claims are made, meriting placement in the top band.

Raw data are tabulated for all 25 trials, means and SDs are given, and a scatter plot with error bars is provided.  Headings and units are mostly clear, though some column labels are mis-aligned and centimetre values are reported as integers despite a stated Â±0.05 cm uncertainty.  Overall, communication is clear but not consistently precise, fitting the 3-4 band.

The student calculates means, standard deviations, skewness and conducts a one-way ANOVA with post-hoc Tukey testsâappropriate techniques for five groups.  Minor numerical inconsistencies (e.g. SD of 12.65 cm vs 14.14 cm in worked example) reveal some inaccuracy, yet the bulk of the processing is correct and relevant, justifying a score in the 3-4 band.

Specific weaknesses are identified (inaccurate soil masses, human reading error, environmental fluctuations, incomplete KCl dissolution).  Their potential influence is mentioned qualitatively but the relative magnitude or direction of each effect is not analysed in depth, matching the descriptor for 3-4.

The student explains findings using accepted physiological concepts (potassiumâs role in photosynthesis, salinity stress from chloride) and cites literature.  The comparison is relevant and extends the data discussion but stops short of a detailed, critical integration with multiple sources; hence the upper middle band is most appropriate.

The research question is clearly stated, identifies both the independent and dependent variables, and is firmly rooted in a specific agronomic context (fertiliser use, basil cultivation, germination period).  Background paragraphs link basil physiology and KCl fertiliser practice, providing explicit justification for the chosen concentrations and 10-day time frame.  This moves well beyond a broad overview, fulfilling the descriptor of âspecific and appropriate context.â

Instrument uncertainties are listed in the materials table and a Â±0.05 cm reading uncertainty is attached to stem length.  However, these uncertainties are not propagated or reflected in the processed data or graph (error bars use SD, not total uncertainty).  The consideration is therefore present but contains significant omissions, matching the middle band.

Proposed improvementsânarrower concentration intervals, longer duration, more frequent treatmentsâare realistic and linked to identified issues, but the student does not thoroughly explain how each change would mitigate the specific errors or improve data quality.  This aligns with the middle-band expectation of âdescribedâ rather than âexplainedâ improvements.

The student identifies numerous variables that could influence basil growth (temperature, soil type, water source, cup size, light) and provides a short justification for each.  The decision to use five KCl levels, five replicates, and a 10-day duration is also rationalised.  While some secondary details (e.g. initial seed viability test) are absent, the overall discussion explains how relevant and sufficient data will be obtained, aligning with the top-band descriptor.

Step-by-step procedures, quantities and uncertainties are supplied, allowing another investigator to reproduce the work.  Nevertheless, there are a few ambiguities: the text alternates between 25 and 40 cups; it is unclear whether seeds were pre-germinated; and initial stem length (time-zero) is not recorded.  These minor omissions place the work at the upper end of the 3-4 band rather than the flawless 5-6 band.

annotations.comment: The controlled variables section (Table 12) only lists the basil source; subsequent variables appear omitted. Ensure all controls are tabulated consistently.
The comprehensive list of environmental and procedural controls in Table 0 details temperature, soil, water, light, and container factors, enhancing experimental validity.
The validity measures mention reliability and controls but omit randomization or blinding to reduce bias. How were trial positions randomized to prevent location effects?
Consider adding a seed viability test in validity measures: pre-test germination rates can identify nonviable seeds and reduce random error in growth measurements.
Procedures omit a time-zero stem length measurement. Recording initial lengths is crucial for calculating true growth increments.
Materials list states 40 seedlings but procedure uses 25 cups; this inconsistency needs resolution to allow reproducibility without ambiguity.
Labeling cups method could be enhanced by including date and replicate codes to prevent misidentification across 10 days of treatment.
The Data Collection Process section outlines qualitative observations but only presents images for 0% treatment, limiting visual comparison of other groups.

The background on basil germination and temperature (Srivastava, 2002; Huang, 2019) effectively situates the research question within a specific physiological context, demonstrating clear relevance to the chosen 10 day timeframe.
The description of basil morphology (Text4) lacks specificity about the cultivar used; linking these traits directly to the sweet basil variety in your experiment would strengthen contextual clarity.
The comparison to the 2023 wheat study introduces useful precedent, but consider adding a pre-germination viability test discussion to ensure seed quality and reduce variability.
The research question is concise and clearly identifies both independent (KCl %) and dependent (stem length) variables within a defined 10 day experimental window.
The percentage formula is based on volumes rather than converting %KCl to grams; clarify how you translate volume% into mass (g) for accurate preparation.
Table 4 neatly consolidates means, standard deviations, and skewness for each treatment, aiding clear comparison across groups.
Incorporating images of seedlings from all treatment levels would strengthen qualitative data and help confirm treatment effects visually.
Raw data table headings and unit placement are misaligned, and integer values obscure the stated Â±0.05 cm measurement uncertainty.
Explain how the reading uncertainty (Â±0.05 cm) was propagated into the mean and standard deviation instead of using SD alone for error bars.
Were zeroâgrowth values treated as true zeros or censored values? Clarify handling of non-germinating trials to justify statistical treatment.
The skewness calculation misapplies the summation step: verify signs and factor of 3 in 2.86 + (â0.36) â 3(â0.36) to ensure accurate skewness.
Verify each term in the skewness equation, especially the cubic transformation of (xâÎ¼)/Ï, to avoid propagation of arithmetic errors.
The inclusion of the full skewness formula and definition of n and xi demonstrates clarity in descriptive analysis presentation.
Skewness entries of NaN for zero-variance groups require discussionâexplain how no-growth data affects assumptions of normality.
Discuss whether treatment groups with NaN skewness still satisfy the normality assumption for subsequent ANOVA testing.
Figure 2.1 error bars are SD only; the caption should note that measurement uncertainty was not propagated through to the graph.
ANOVA table omits the F-statistic and degrees of freedom; include these values in Table 6 for completeness.
Adding the F-ratio and its associated degrees of freedom would clarify how the p-value was derived in the ANOVA test.
Was homogeneity of variances verified (e.g., Leveneâs test) prior to ANOVA? Reporting this ensures assumptions are met.
The post-hoc Tukey HSD table comprehensively lists pairwise comparisons, HSD thresholds, and Q values, facilitating transparent inference.
Many post-hoc p-values are shown as 0.00000; report these as p < 0.0001 or the smallest threshold to avoid misleading precision.
Highlight which Q values exceed critical Q.05 or Q.01 thresholds in the narrative to interpret strength of pairwise differences.
The conclusion succinctly addresses the research question, aligns with statistical outcomes, and confirms the hypothesis in quantitative terms.
The conclusion claims no surprising or outlying results but overlooks variation in SD and skewness; reflect on data spread to contextualize findings.
The Errors and Limitations section lists methodological weaknesses but does not assess their relative influence on the results.
Quantify or rank the listed errors (e.g., measurement vs environmental) to explain which had the greatest impact on data validity.
Improvement ideas are realistic but lack direct linkage to identified limitations; explain how each proposed change mitigates specific weaknesses.

rubricCriterionStrandBandContents.content: No work presented or work does not reach the standard described.
No work presented or work does not reach the standard described.
No work presented or work does not reach the standard described.
The research question is stated without context.
Methodological considerations associated with collecting data relevant to the research question are stated.
The description of the methodology for collecting or selecting data lacks the detail to allow for the investigation to be reproduced.
The description of the methodology for collecting or selecting data allows for the investigation to be reproduced with few ambiguities or omissions.
The research question is outlined within a broad context.
Methodological considerations associated with collecting relevant and sufficient data to answer the research question are described.
The research question is described within a specific and appropriate context.
Methodological considerations associated with collecting relevant and sufficient data to answer the research question are explained.
The description of the methodology for collecting or selecting data allows for the investigation to be reproduced.
No work presented or work does not reach the standard described.
No work presented or work does not reach the standard described.
No work presented or work does not reach the standard described.
The recording and processing of the data is communicated but is neither clear nor precise.
Some processing of data relevant to addressing the research question is carried out but with major omissions, inaccuracies or inconsistencies.
The recording and processing of data shows limited evidence of the consideration of uncertainties.
The processing of data relevant to addressing the research question is carried out but with some significant omissions, inaccuracies or inconsistencies.
The communication of the recording and processing of the data is either clear or precise.
The recording and processing of data shows evidence of a consideration of uncertainties but with some significant omissions or inaccuracies.
The processing of data relevant to addressing the research question is carried out appropriately and accurately.
The recording and processing of data shows evidence of an appropriate consideration of uncertainties.
The communication of the recording and processing of the data is both clear and precise.

A conclusion is described that makes some relevant comparison to the accepted scientific context.
A conclusion is described that is relevant to the research question but is not fully consistent with the analysis presented.
A conclusion is justified that is relevant to the research question and fully consistent with the analysis presented.
A conclusion is justified through relevant comparison to the accepted scientific context.
No work presented or work does not reach the standard described.
No work presented or work does not reach the standard described.
Realistic improvements to the investigation are stated.
The report states generic methodological weaknesses or limitations.
Realistic improvements to the investigation that are relevant to the identified weaknesses or limitations, are described.
The report describes specific methodological weaknesses or limitations.
Realistic improvements to the investigation, that are relevant to the identified weaknesses or limitations, are explained.
The report explains the relative impact of specific methodological weaknesses or limitations.
